b' Zaz\xe2\x80\x99s M&A Options, The Bidenpocalypse & Ken Griffin\xe2\x80\x99s New Scalp
Welcome back to Dry Powder. I\xe2\x80\x99m Bill Cohan, and I\xe2\x80\x99m not running for president. In today\xe2\x80\x99s issue, an informed meditation on the hostile M&A environment that David Zaslav countenances as he ponders analyst Jessica Reif Ehrlich\xe2\x80\x99s dire report, earlier this week, suggesting that WBD\xe2\x80\x99s \xe2\x80\x9ccurrent composition as a consolidated public company is not working.\xe2\x80\x9d \xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c

Welcome back to Dry Powder. I\xe2\x80\x99m Bill Cohan , and I\xe2\x80\x99m not running for president.

In today\xe2\x80\x99s issue, an informed meditation on the hostile M&A environment that David Zaslav countenances as he ponders analyst Jessica Reif Ehrlich \xe2\x80\x99s dire report, earlier this week, suggesting that WBD\xe2\x80\x99s \xe2\x80\x9ccurrent composition as a consolidated public company is not working.\xe2\x80\x9d

But first\xe2\x80\xa6

  • Post-Biden : Of course, the biggest story in America right now is the announcement that Joe Biden has ended his reelection campaign and endorsed Kamala Harris to replace him as the Democratic presidential nominee. Like many of you, I\xe2\x80\x99ve been captivated by my Puck partners\xe2\x80\x99 brilliant reportage in and around Biden and Trump world, from Tara Palmeri \xe2\x80\x99s masterful Fall of the Biden Bunker to Julia Ioffe \xe2\x80\x99s Biden Group Therapy and John Heilemann \xe2\x80\x99s Trump\xe2\x80\x99s 25 Paths to 270 . It\xe2\x80\x99s also worth revisiting Peter Hamby \xe2\x80\x99s prescient The Kamala Calculus .
    In order to make sure you don\xe2\x80\x99t miss any of their essential reporting during this consequential and pivotal week, click here to subscribe to Puck\xe2\x80\x99s The Best & The Brightest private email.

    For my part, I have to concede this was the right decision for the country, but could not have been an easy one for Biden. I guess that means we\xe2\x80\x99ve seen two rather dramatic acts of presidential courage in the last week or so. Here\xe2\x80\x99s to hoping the Democrats can rally behind a vigorous, progressive ticket and keep control of the White House in November.

  • What do you get for a guy who already has a copy of the Constitution? : Ken Griffin likes to buy very expensive things and loan them to museums. When he spent $500 million buying two paintings from David Geffen \xe2\x80\x94 Willem de Kooning \xe2\x80\x99s Interchange and Jackson Pollock \xe2\x80\x99s Number 17A \xe2\x80\x94he loaned them to the Art Institute of Chicago. Recently, he spent $43 million on an early copy of the U.S. Constitution and loaned it to Crystal Bridges Museum of American Art in Arkansas. Now he\xe2\x80\x99s paid $44 million for a stegosaurus skeleton found nearly intact in Colorado. The 150 million-year-old animal has been nicknamed \xe2\x80\x9cApex.\xe2\x80\x9d No word yet on which museum Griffin will loan it to, but presumably he won\xe2\x80\x99t be keeping it in his foyer.


  • Jessica in her own words : And finally, in case you missed it, Jessica Reif Ehrlich joined my partner Matt Belloni on his fantastic Hollywood podcast, The Town , last week for a candid discussion of her dagger of an analyst report from Tuesday, in which she posits that WBD\xe2\x80\x99s strategy \xe2\x80\x9cis not working\xe2\x80\x9d and that \xe2\x80\x9cexploring strategic alternatives, such as asset sales, restructuring and/or mergers would create more shareholder value vs. the status quo.\xe2\x80\x9d You can stream the episode here , or check out Matt\xe2\x80\x99s excellent column on Zaz\xe2\x80\x99s complex thicket here , and make sure you never miss his incredible work by signing up for What I\xe2\x80\x99m Hearing .

Speaking of which\xe2\x80\xa6

Zaz\xe2\x80\x99s Everything-on-the-Table Era
On the heels of the now-infamous BofA note suggesting that WBD needs to buy or sell assets to survive, and Zaz\xe2\x80\x99s own misguided political commentary at the Lodge in Sun Valley, it\xe2\x80\x99s increasingly clear that both the markets and Zaz know that his company needs a shake-up. But what to do in an environment so inhospitable to M&A?
WILLIAM D. COHAN
If you talk to David Zaslav , as I do on occasion, there\xe2\x80\x99s a good chance he\xe2\x80\x99ll eventually bring up the penultimate scene in Moneyball , the film adaptation of my friend Michael Lewis \xe2\x80\x99s classic book, where Billy Beane (played by Brad Pitt ) spends time at Fenway Park with my other friend John Henry (played by Arliss Howard ), the Red Sox\xe2\x80\x99s then-new principal owner. In the scene, Henry wants to hire Beane as the Red Sox\xe2\x80\x99s general manager because he believes his radical reliance on data-based decision-making, rather than baseball\xe2\x80\x99s old dogmas and gut instincts, are what his team needs to deliver a championship to Boston for the first time since 1918, and to finally purge the Curse of the Bambino. And yet, the fictional Henry concedes that he knows it won\xe2\x80\x99t be easy: Beane\xe2\x80\x99s philosophy would upend baseball traditions and people\xe2\x80\x99s livelihoods. \xe2\x80\x9cIt\xe2\x80\x99s threatening their jobs,\xe2\x80\x9d Henry says. \xe2\x80\x9cIt\xe2\x80\x99s threatening the way that they do things\xe2\x80\xa6\xe2\x80\x9d

Just like in baseball then, there\xe2\x80\x99s a generational disruption going on in Hollywood now, and it seems like Zaz is trying to do whatever he can, given the current restrictions, to make sure that WBD is one of the survivors of the creative destruction now underway, even if it means upending people\xe2\x80\x99s livelihoods. And thanks to my partner Dylan Byers \xe2\x80\x99 crackerjack reporting from the front lines of Big Media, we got a snippet of his thinking from last week\xe2\x80\x99s Allen & Co. conference, in Sun Valley, albeit expressed somewhat inelegantly. Dylan reported that Zaz said he didn\xe2\x80\x99t care necessarily who won the upcoming presidential election. Instead, he cared about whether the next president would be friendly to business interests. \xe2\x80\x9cWe just need an opportunity for deregulation,\xe2\x80\x9d Zaz said at the Lodge, \xe2\x80\x9cso companies can consolidate and do what we need to to be even better.\xe2\x80\x9d

A MESSAGE FROM OUR SPONSOR
Are You Prepared for Sunsetting Estate Tax Exemptions?

Historically high estate tax exemptions are scheduled to sunset at the end of 2025. These rates present an important opportunity \xe2\x80\x94 but rushing to plan can lead to costly mistakes. Our experts can help you model how tax changes and gifting will impact your goals, liquidity and overall financial plan.

Start Now.
On the one hand, this is the kind of comment you would expect the C.E.O. of a $60 billion enterprise-value company to make. C.E.O.s of big companies like to be able to make strategic moves in order to stay competitive, especially when their industry is being bombarded by new competition and macroeconomic factors that are crushing profits and restricting their maneuverability. The last thing they want, in those circumstances, is a regulatory environment that makes it difficult for them to flex. And as everyone who attends the Sun Valley conference knows only too well, that\xe2\x80\x99s what the Biden administration has confronted them with by putting Lina Khan in charge of the Federal Trade Commission.

But what made Zaz\xe2\x80\x99s comments so poignant, I think, is that he\xe2\x80\x99s a lifelong Democrat who grew up in a two-bedroom apartment in Brooklyn. Yes, he\xe2\x80\x99s come a long way since then\xe2\x80\x94he owns the requisite homes in East Hampton and Beverly Hills and his various boards of directors have paid him a monumental fortune over the years\xe2\x80\x94but he\xe2\x80\x99s still a Democrat who supports the Biden administration and speaks regularly to Gina Raimondo , the Commerce Secretary. (Earlier today, obviously, Biden announced he wasn\xe2\x80\x99t running for reelection. I\xe2\x80\x99d still expect Zaz to support whoever replaces Biden at the top of the ticket.)

Moreover, two months after the expiry of the Reverse Morris restrictions on Zaz\xe2\x80\x99s merger of Discovery Communications and the WarnerMedia assets, it suggests that Zaz is looking to deal, collaborate, explore\xe2\x80\x94anything, in fact, to boost his stock price. And the comments were magnified and crystallized by two other events last week, as my partner Matt Belloni has brilliantly noted : BofA Securities analyst Jessica Reif Ehrlich \xe2\x80\x99s report that WBD\xe2\x80\x99s \xe2\x80\x9ccurrent composition as a consolidated public company is not working,\xe2\x80\x9d and a Financial Times piece that mysteriously entered the inner monologue of WBD leadership, suggesting various spin-off options, like sending the cable assets off to sea with a bunch of debt, and focusing on a streamer-and-studio growthco. (I don\xe2\x80\x99t see it, personally, for the simple reason that there would be a debt-holder meltdown, even if WBD\xe2\x80\x99s cov-lite loans might permit the rearrangement of the security packages.)

If the government won\xe2\x80\x99t let companies like WBD do any deals, then what choice does Zaz have to try to create shareholder value other than by breaking up his own company? Which, I have to say, kind of defeats the purpose behind putting it together in the first place. (Maybe I\xe2\x80\x99m biased as an old Wall Street M&A guy. I know most M&A deals don\xe2\x80\x99t work out, but I usually respect the industrial logic for doing them.) In any event, since Zaz made his comment in Sun Valley, the WBD stock has had one of its best runs in a long, long time\xe2\x80\x94up 18 percent in the past week, although it\xe2\x80\x99s still down 65 percent from the start of this experiment in April 2022.
Deal or No Deal
This isn\xe2\x80\x99t investment advice, but I don\xe2\x80\x99t get the sense that Zaz is looking to break anything up. Instead, I think he\xe2\x80\x99s going to be partner-curious until he can pull the trigger on something larger. I think that\xe2\x80\x99s what he wants to do, and I think it\xe2\x80\x99s what he has to do, too.

I\xe2\x80\x99m sure, for instance, that Zaz could have done the Paramount deal if he\xe2\x80\x99d wanted to\xe2\x80\x94he probably would have had the financial firepower to get it done, and his banks would have supported him by providing the needed financing. Of course, that\xe2\x80\x99s not the same as saying that the WBD-Paramount Global deal would have been advisable\xe2\x80\x94quite the opposite, in fact. (A conclusion that Zaz no doubt reached himself.) As I have written before, hoovering up Paramount\xe2\x80\x99s $12 billion of debt would have been the last thing WBD needed after spending the last two years reducing its debt load by $15 billion, to say nothing of inheriting Paramount\xe2\x80\x99s other problems, such as the $2 billion in losses at Paramount+ and the declining fortunes of its linear TV businesses.

But all of that aside\xe2\x80\x94and that\xe2\x80\x99s not to minimize those challenges\xe2\x80\x94it has to give a C.E.O. pause to pull the trigger on a deal like Paramount when he or she knows that regulators are going to tie the company up in knots for 18 months while the deal gets analyzed and then may or may not approve it. Who needs that hassle? And what board of directors will underwrite that risk? Things are moving way too fast in the land of Big Media and streaming to be bogged down for 18 months for any reason.

So no wonder Zaz is pleading for some relief and a faster decision process, and a signal that such deals are at least within the realm of possibility. And maybe some relief is coming, in this very specific area , in the form of a second Trump administration. I suspect that\xe2\x80\x99s what, say, Google is counting on with its likely $23 billion acquisition of Wiz. It seems unlikely that Google would attempt that deal if it didn\xe2\x80\x99t think the antitrust and regulatory environment in Washington was closer to being changed than not.

So what\xe2\x80\x99s a Zaz to do? In the absence of being able to do material M&A deals, I think what we\xe2\x80\x99re seeing now in Hollywood is a new age of collaboration and partnerships. That\xe2\x80\x99s looking like the only way these big players who have brought us billions of dollars of fresh streaming content can hope to stem the losses they are facing. That\xe2\x80\x99s why WBD, Disney, and Fox joined together to create their sports bundle, Venu, in May, and why Zaz pursued the Hulu, Max, and Disney+ bundle (the details of which are still to come). I wouldn\xe2\x80\x99t be surprised to see Paramount+ somehow, at some point, come together with Max. How else is David Ellison and RedBird Capital going to get the $2 billion in losses at Paramount+ off the Paramount Global balance sheet? I know Larry Ellison is one of the richest men in the world, but even he can\xe2\x80\x99t countenance a business that loses $2 billion a year.

I\xe2\x80\x99m sure on some level that Zaz would like to be able to buy Fox Corporation from the Murdochs . If he did, or could, he\xe2\x80\x99d get a coveted package of NFL games and a bunch of decent Fox programming. (He\xe2\x80\x99d have to do something with Fox News and Fox Business, I suspect, given how poorly they would combine with CNN, for a variety of reasons.) But unless something changes in Washington, he won\xe2\x80\x99t be able to do that deal. What he will be able to do, and apparently has done, is get in a room with Bob Iger and Lachlan Murdoch \xe2\x80\x94people he knows, likes, and trusts, and who know, like, and trust him\xe2\x80\x94and cut a deal to create Venu. Nor would I be surprised to see Zaz collaborate more frequently with Neal Mohan , the C.E.O. of YouTube, to bring WBD\xe2\x80\x99s content to YouTube\xe2\x80\x99s audience. They already have the Sunday Ticket-Max deal together and collaborated on a roughly $20 billion bid for the NBA rights package that seems to be headed to Amazon. (It didn\xe2\x80\x99t work out in the end.) The Mohan/Zaz friendship is real, at least if their time together in Sun Valley is any indication. This is the kind of thing the remaining Hollywood players have to do since the regulatory environment is so difficult for them. They are collaborating out of necessity; it\xe2\x80\x99s akin to a presidential executive order when the dynamics in Congress prevent the passage of legislation.
The Long-Dated Maturities
The real fear in Hollywood these days is what\xe2\x80\x99s coming\xe2\x80\x94and is, in fact, already here\xe2\x80\x94from the tech companies, such as Apple, Amazon, and Google, to say nothing of Netflix. Apple, Amazon, and Google have infinite resources, of course, and their focus is not on creating traditional Hollywood content, per se , so much as getting customers into their ecosystems so that they can sell them what they really want to sell them. Zaz is probably worried that the trillion-dollar tech companies, studying their algorithms and their data streams, will be the only survivors of the content wars\xe2\x80\x94even if it\xe2\x80\x99s just a peripheral business for them\xe2\x80\x94while the traditional Hollywood players, who care about movie quality and the shared experience of moviegoing, are kicked to the curb, suffering from mounting losses and fewer degrees of M&A freedom.

Maybe this is just a real-time example of what economist Joseph Schumpeter meant when he described the \xe2\x80\x9ccreative destruction\xe2\x80\x9d inherent in capitalism. The market isn\xe2\x80\x99t exactly seeing these traditional mediacos as survivors. As I said above, WBD\xe2\x80\x99s stock is down 65 percent since Zaz formed the company. Disney\xe2\x80\x99s stock is down 32 percent in the past five years. Comcast\xe2\x80\x99s stock is down a more modest 9 percent in the past five years, thanks probably to its lucrative cable and theme park businesses. Paramount Global\xe2\x80\x99s stock is down 77 percent in the past five years, and that\xe2\x80\x99s after the Ellison buyout agreement. Meanwhile, Netflix\xe2\x80\x99s stock is up 101 percent in the past five years\xe2\x80\x94 Bill Ackman should never have sold out\xe2\x80\x94Apple\xe2\x80\x99s stock is up 342 percent in the past five years, and on and on. It\xe2\x80\x99s truly a tale of winners and losers.

And yet, I think Zaz still cares about creativity, and that\xe2\x80\x99s what prompted his comments at Sun Valley. I think he\xe2\x80\x99s fighting to save the structure of the old-fashioned movie studio system. It might well be a losing battle, whether or not Trump wins, but Zaz still has some time, given the free cash flow WBD is now generating and the long-dated maturities on the WBD debt stack. As Zaz is fond of saying to his team at WBD, \xe2\x80\x9cOur job isn\xe2\x80\x99t to do what Wall Street thinks is right. Our job isn\xe2\x80\x99t to make Hollywood happy. Our job is to build a great creative company and do what we think is right to make this company successful for the future. And if people are unhappy or people don\xe2\x80\x99t like you and don\xe2\x80\x99t like me, you know, that\xe2\x80\x99s okay.\xe2\x80\x9d

In the final scene in Moneyball , we learn that Beane turned down Henry\xe2\x80\x99s offer to become the Red Sox general manager. He stayed with the Oakland A\xe2\x80\x99s, which all these years later still haven\xe2\x80\x99t won another World Series. Two years later, though, using Beane\xe2\x80\x99s philosophy, or some of it anyway, the Red Sox finally won the World Series for the first time in 86 years. No wonder Zaz likes that movie and shows it to his direct reports. It has a happy ending, sort of.
